main_summary.summary: Barbara Edna Vickers (Mrs. Adell Vickers) was interviewed by Joe Mosnier on September 13, 2011, at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine, Florida, for the Civil Rights History Project. Vickers recounts her upbringing in St. Augustine, where she attended St. Benedict the Moor Catholic School and Excelsior High School, graduating in 1942. After leaving St. Augustine to escape its racial caste system, she worked as a tack welder in shipyards during World War II in New York and Seattle (briefly at Boeing Aircraft as a mechanic helper). She returned to St. Augustine after marrying Adell Vickers and opened a beauty salon. In the early 1960s she joined the movement led by her across-the-street neighbor Dr. Robert B. Hayling, participating in kneel-ins at Grace United Methodist Church, traveling to Tallahassee to petition the governor, and serving on the Scott Street network that monitored Klan night-rides and protected Hayling's home. She describes the September 1963 Klan shooting attack on the Hayling residence in which his dogs Madonna and a boxer were killed. The interview's second half documents her decade-long campaign as president of the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Remembrance Committee to build the Foot Soldiers Monument on the central plaza, raising roughly $70,000 with major support from Phil McDaniel, attorney Patrick Canan, Hank Thomas, and a donor she names as Nina Verilam.
key_themes: Dr. Robert B. Hayling and the Scott Street network
Kneel-ins, sit-ins, and swim-ins as direct-action tactics
Klan night-riding and the 1963 attack on Hayling's home
Black women's labor in WWII shipyards
Foot Soldiers Monument and civil rights commemoration
Interracial coalition-building in the monument campaign
main_summary.historical_significance:
Vickers's testimony is a first-person account of the St. Augustine campaign of 1963-1964, one of the SCLC's most violent and least-publicly-memorialized confrontations, and the immediate prelude to the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Her recollection of being recruited by Dr. King in person to participate in church kneel-ins, of the Scott Street mutual-defense network that guarded Hayling's home, and of the September 1963 Klan shooting that killed Hayling's dogs adds grassroots detail to a campaign more commonly narrated through King, Andrew Young, and Ralph Abernathy.

The second half of the interview documents the founding of the Foot Soldiers Monument (dedicated 2011) on St. Augustine's central plaza, the first civil rights monument in a city that, by Vickers's account and Mosnier's framing, had spent decades erasing its movement history from public view. Her decade-long fundraising effort, the fact that most major donations came from white attorneys and businesspeople rather than the local Black church community, and her successful insistence on commemorating the rank-and-file foot soldiers rather than King alone constitute a case study in civil rights memorialization politics.

chapters.title: Interview Opening and Setting
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern oral history program opens the interview at the Bayfront Inn in St. Augustine, Florida, on September 13, 2011, for the Civil Rights History Project. Dr. Robert Hayling is present as a fellow series interviewee, and Mosnier frames the session around St. Augustine's movement history through the monument.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project oral history series, 2011

chapters.title: Schooling at St. Benedict and Excelsior
chapters.summary: Vickers describes being raised in St. Augustine, attending the parochial St. Benedict Catholic school near her home through eighth grade, then graduating from Excelsior High School, the only Black high school in the area, around 1942.

chapters.title: Parents Working the Hotels
chapters.summary: Vickers recalls that both parents did hotel work. Her mother waitressed at the hotel that was then the Monson Motor Lodge, riding a bike to work, while her father was the head waiter at the Bennett Hotel a few doors down and the only one in the family with a car. She remembers her mother, grandmother, and aunt all working there in their uniforms.

chapters.title: Her Sisters Gwendolyn and Charlotte
chapters.summary: Vickers explains that she was the oldest sister, nine years older than Gwendolyn and nineteen years older than the baby sister Charlotte. She mentions losing the sister beneath her about two years before the interview and says she and her surviving sister talk every day.

chapters.title: Leaving a Racist St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Vickers describes her urgency to leave St. Augustine after finishing school, calling it a racist town. She recounts having to move to the back of the grocery line when white customers entered and being forced off the sidewalk, and her resolve to find a better life anywhere else, intending never to return except to visit her parents.

chapters.title: New York Shipyard and Tack Welding
chapters.summary: Vickers recounts moving to New York right after high school, around 1942, and staying about four years. With wartime demand for welders, she was sent to school and learned to tack weld, then got a job on a ship. Being from the South among all white men who first ignored her greetings, she kept saying good morning until she had the whole crew laughing and greeting her first.

chapters.title: Seattle and Boeing Aircraft
chapters.summary: Vickers recalls leaving for Seattle expecting to tack weld at Boeing Aircraft, with the New York crew sorry to see her go. In Seattle she found she was being used as a mechanic helper rather than a welder. She accepted it and worked the graveyard shift because she disliked early mornings.

chapters.title: Corrine and the Train to Seattle
chapters.summary: Vickers tells the story of persuading her friend Corrine to leave a job she liked and travel with her to Seattle when Vickers was about nineteen. Corrine missed the train, leaving Vickers in tears, but a telegram asked her to wait in Chicago. Reunited there, Vickers rode the El for the first time before they continued on to Seattle together.

chapters.title: Marriage and Return to St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Vickers describes coming back to St. Augustine after marrying Adell Vickers, who was from the city and devoted to it. He worked as a bellhop, and after they married he insisted they live in St. Augustine, giving her a week to follow him back. She gave up a good job and returned in the late 1950s, just before the civil rights movement broke out there.
related_events: Post-WWII return migration to the South

chapters.title: Dr. King Calls Her to Serve
chapters.summary: Vickers recalls Dr. Robert B. Hayling as a neighbor across the street whose rallies and meetings she attended. The night Dr. King came to the church, Hayling said he needed people to go to the churches, and King looked at her and asked, young lady, will you go. She said yes, then immediately wondered what she had committed to.

chapters.title: Sent Church to Church for Kneel-Ins
chapters.summary: Vickers describes her first kneel-ins, going to churches and being sent by the two young white men to report back to Hayling's office. When she thought she had done her duty and could go home, Hayling told her to go to the next church and the next, and that is how she became fully involved in every meeting.

chapters.title: The Scott Street Defense Network
chapters.summary: Vickers describes Scott Street, eleven houses whose residents each had a job in the kneel-ins, sit-ins, and swim-ins. Julia Conway, Purcell Conway's mother, organized them into a group to protect Hayling, and the West Side Boys took positions in the trees around his house. Vickers worried at his fearlessness while he stayed active in the street.

chapters.title: Phone Alerts When the Klan Rode Through
chapters.summary: Vickers describes the network the Scott Street residents formed to call one another when the Klan came through the street. They warned each other to get down because the riders were shooting low, and people would crawl from the refrigerator and open its door for cover. It was real scary, she says, as she and the others kept watch to make sure Hayling got into the house safely.
related_events: 1963 Klan attacks on St. Augustine movement

chapters.title: A 3 a.m. Ride and a Night Deposit
chapters.summary: Vickers recounts the night her aunt was sick and her car would not start, so she asked Hayling for a ride home around 3 a.m. Instead of driving straight back, he went downtown to make his night deposit with her in the car, telling her plainly he had to do it in case he was not there the next morning. She never rode with him again.

chapters.title: The Shooting at Hayling's Home
chapters.summary: Vickers describes how the group cooked meals for the boys protecting Hayling, but the night of the shooting most of them had fallen asleep when gunfire hit his home. Arriving back, she found an officer blocking Scott Street, and Julia Conway said she had given the heavyset cop a chair. Hayling's dogs, including Madonna and a boxer, were killed.

chapters.title: Sending the Children to Tallahassee
chapters.summary: Vickers recalls that after the attack Hayling needed friends to keep his children until he could take them to Tallahassee, and a next-door neighbor offered to keep the girls. His wife, pregnant with their third child, had just gotten off a sofa struck by bullets. Vickers reflects that no one was safe, and Hayling took the children away because there was no telling what would happen.

chapters.title: The Drive to Petition the Governor
chapters.summary: Vickers describes traveling to Tallahassee with Hayling to talk to the governor, going because the movement wanted people from different churches and she was Catholic. She calls it another scary ride, watching behind her for cars that might shoot, recalling how drivers ran them off the road heading to Butler's Beach. They spoke with the governor but it did little good at the time.
related_events: St. Augustine movement appeals to state government

chapters.title: Her Salon and Her Husband's Work
chapters.summary: Asked what she and her husband did for work, Vickers explains she was a beautician with her own home salon, licensed to operate from the house. Adell Vickers worked at a drive-in movie theater and was a skilled gambler who always had money, though she disliked that he had no steady job. Solomon Calhoun, her former high school basketball coach, later got him a position at the Recreation Center.

chapters.title: Her Husband, Father Figure to the Kids
chapters.summary: Vickers describes her husband as recreation director and a father figure who put children first, once buying a boy shoes so he could play softball. Mothers asked him to find their children, and he always knew where they hung out, scolding them and sending them home. The children answered him with respect.

chapters.title: Keeping the Salon Open Under Threat
chapters.summary: Asked how she managed through the stress and violence, Vickers explains she hung blankets in the shop so no light showed outside, since most customers were afraid to be seen there, just as Hayling's patients feared coming to his office. She got by because Adell Vickers was a good provider, and she enjoyed the work anyway, often not charging mothers with several children at Easter even when some con customers never brought back the money they owed.

chapters.title: The Klan and the Tire Iron Arrest Threat
chapters.summary: Asked whether the Klan marched openly down Scott Street, Vickers says they marched in Lincolnville and came through Scott Street like bullies, shooting and intimidating. She recounts coming from a rally when a cop stopped her, searched her trunk, and called the tire iron a weapon he could arrest her for, leaving her nervous and powerless.

chapters.title: A Drive to Be Part of the Change
chapters.summary: Vickers describes still going back and forth every night despite a car that broke down on every corner, driven by a need to witness the meetings herself rather than trust secondhand accounts. She recalls that after Hayling finally brought Dr. King and King asked her to participate in the churches, she thought that if there was going to be a change, this was the time, and people could not just sit and wait but had to take part.
related_events: King's involvement in the St. Augustine movement

chapters.title: Her Grandmother Begged Her to Stop
chapters.summary: Vickers describes her family's fear: one sister was afraid and away at Bethune-Cookman College in Daytona, and her terminally ill grandmother tried to keep her from going. Watching the protests on TV, her grandmother warned the activism would get her killed and said white people would never let them eat together. Vickers answered that they wanted to be free and pay their money at the restaurant, and told her plainly she was going anyway because the time was right.

chapters.title: No Children of Her Own Yet
chapters.summary: Asked whether she had children at the time, Vickers says she had a son she inherited at five years old, now a certified professional and the best thing that ever happened to her, but she did not have children of her own during the movement years and so was not also watching over them.

chapters.title: Refusing the Life of a Maid
chapters.summary: Asked for her most vivid memories, Vickers reflects on the few who were genuinely evil and on her refusal to do housework for white families. She describes the humiliation of having to enter through the back door to fetch a newspaper from the front porch, and says she never wanted to live that kind of life, which is partly why she went to New York and did factory work.

chapters.title: Mrs. Hayling and the Family's Strain
chapters.summary: After a short break, Vickers describes Mrs. Hayling as a very quiet, sweet, low-key person rarely seen outside, appearing mainly when she brought her two little girls across the street for hair care. Reflecting on the family's strain, Vickers recounts meeting Mrs. Hayling later in Fort Lauderdale at Christmas, where the traumatized woman kept crossing the street until Vickers identified herself, then explained a relative had just been robbed and she was trying to get the dogs home.

chapters.title: Missing the First Visit With Dr. King
chapters.summary: Asked about further contact with Dr. King, Vickers explains that the first time he came, she and Hayling set up business owners to meet him, but as president of the beautician association she had to attend a convention in Daytona instead. She asked Queenie Mitchell McCall to represent the beauticians, telling her King was not yet famous, and only later regretted missing the chance.

chapters.title: Shaking Dr. King's Hand
chapters.summary: Vickers describes the night she finally met King, arriving late because of a customer and finding no room at St. Paul AME Church, so she went to First Baptist where Reverend Abernathy was speaking. King came over afterward, and everyone got to shake his hand. She remembers his hands as soft as cotton and his reputation for never forgetting a name.

chapters.title: The Insurance Man at Grace Methodist
chapters.summary: Asked how she responded to being turned away at kneel-ins, Vickers recounts going to Grace Methodist Church, where a white Metropolitan Life insurance agent who collected her premium every Monday stood over her kneeling form with his foot positioned as if to kick. When he came to her door the next day she warned him never to return and dropped the policy, refusing to talk to him after he had treated her that way at his church.

chapters.title: Why They Call Her a Fanatic
chapters.summary: Vickers compares the era to the film The Help, telling her niece that people really did live that way and that mothers with babies to feed had to step out of line and wait to get baby food. She says people call her a fanatic about civil rights, but she felt good knowing they would finally have a chance and that the children of today would not have to endure all of that.
related_events: Civil rights and voting rights legacy

chapters.title: What the Monument Means to Her
chapters.summary: Vickers expresses disbelief that people are not donating to help build the monument, while her sister explains they simply do not feel about it the way she does. To Vickers the monument means the world because it recognizes everyone who came, not only Hayling, Dr. Young, and Dr. King, but the people who put their lives on the line, including college students and white participants who gave up money and safety.

chapters.title: Insisting on a Foot Soldiers Monument
chapters.summary: Vickers explains that the monument idea came to her about ten years earlier, when other groups were discussing a King monument rather than one for the foot soldiers. She wanted to honor the rank-and-file participants, and when she talked to Dr. Hayling he urged her to settle for a plaque or marker. She refused, insisting they would get a full monument.

chapters.title: Organizing Through the Council on Aging
chapters.summary: Asked how she began organizing, Vickers describes the Council on Aging, where director Kathy Brown formed a Mayor's Senior Advisory Committee to advise the city. After the first president quit, Vickers was made president and kept the effort alive, correcting a sign-up process that had been blocking open meetings and pressing forward across changes in the mayor's office.

chapters.title: Choosing the Foot Soldiers Over King
chapters.summary: Vickers tells Kathy Brown her dream of a foot soldiers monument, and Brown advises that a Martin Luther King monument would draw far better participation and get done quickly. Vickers insists she wants the foot soldiers, the people who really put their lives on the line, and resolves to organize her own group and secure 501(c) status to make it happen.

chapters.title: First Donations and a UPS Grant
chapters.summary: Vickers recounts that a donor she names as Nina Verilam called and, asking what it would take to get started, pledged the roughly $20,000 needed to retain the sculptor, leaving Vickers speechless. Other donations came in, and her grandson, who works for UPS, pursued a company grant that ultimately came through at around $7,400, convincing her there was no stopping the effort.

chapters.title: Signing the Sculptor and Raising More
chapters.summary: Vickers explains the budget reached roughly $70,000, up from an early estimate near $60,000. The sculptor came to the plaza to sign the contract and received $20,000 to begin. A supporter named Phil McDaniel told her she was almost there, and when she said they needed at least another $20,000, he promised to raise it in one night.

chapters.title: The Dinner at Patrick Canan's Office
chapters.summary: Vickers describes meeting at attorney Patrick Canan's office, where she told the civil rights story and her reason for honoring the foot soldiers. Canan had his secretary invite guests at $100 a head, asking only that she bring people who took part in the movement to speak and provide a little music. She recruited Del Kahn to play and Freddie Thompson, who had been under the hedges with her the night Andrew Young was beaten and the rocks kept coming.

chapters.title: Only One Black Church Gave
chapters.summary: Vickers recounts that the dinner raised $24,000 and the money seemed to keep coming, yet she was upset, telling her sister she could not understand why. She had expected every Black church in St. Augustine to help, but only one small, poor church sent $100. When Phil invited the American Legion, which she had pursued in vain, they finally came through with their money.

chapters.title: Bringing the Monument to the Plaza
chapters.summary: Mosnier frames how little St. Augustine had done to mark its civil rights history, and asks how Vickers navigated placing a prominent monument on the central plaza. She describes going to the City Commission with a proposal while two rival groups feuded over their own monument ideas. She left one feuding group, joined another, then organized fresh after backers doubted it would work, eventually proceeding with Kathy.

chapters.title: The City's Slow and Late Help
chapters.summary: Asked about the city government's reaction, Vickers says the city was very slow and did not help as she had hoped it might. City Commissioner Earl Jones, who was African American, said he had $5,000 allotted for downtown work and would give it, though it came down to the city using the money for prep work at the last minute. The city later paid for the walkway with footsteps.

chapters.title: Where the Money Came From
chapters.summary: Vickers credits good rapport from the mayor, city manager, and city attorney on legal questions, even as she notes the senior advisory effort still has far to go. Nearly all the money raised came from the white business community, with very little from the Black community beyond small individual gifts. Hank Thomas of the Freedom Riders gave a sizable donation, while major support came from Phil McDaniel, Patrick Canan, and the white attorneys they gathered.

chapters.title: Overcome at the Installation
chapters.summary: Asked how the installation day felt, Vickers says she just lost it as it was happening. Twelve family members came from Fort Lauderdale, and though they said she did not seem nervous, she was. Worn down by the long campaign, she was lost for words when called to speak, so she waved the crowd off and they laughed and applauded. Hayling then quietly credits her with the tremendous organization and determination that carried the monument through.

chapters.title: A Monument to Define the City
chapters.summary: After the break, Mosnier reflects as an outsider that giving this history a physical presence through a piece of art on the central plaza is a significant act that may carry long and important implications for the city in the years to come. Vickers answers simply that she hopes so.

chapters.title: How the Work Changed Her View
chapters.summary: Invited to share final thoughts, Vickers says the years of work on the committee changed her perspective on white people, having carried a bitter taste and expecting their true colors to turn. Instead the effort brought closeness with people who took part in the movement, including her enduring friendship with Dr. Hayling, which means a great deal to her.

chapters.title: At 88, Still Ready to Go
chapters.summary: Vickers invokes her grandmother's stories of people dragged from their homes, lynched, and caught after dark in the plaza, measuring her own experience against that history. She says there is still so much to do, that neither she nor Hayling can do it all, but they can keep young people motivated, and at 88 she is still ready to go. Mosnier thanks her and closes the interview.

loc_video.caption: Barbara Edna Vickers oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 13
